_note: Reverse-generated from src/topics/rpc/RpcDemo.jsx + rpcText.json. Use with /sim-from-spec or specs/SIMULATION-SKILL.md to regenerate or create similar simulations.

meta.title: RPC (Remote Procedure Call)
meta.description: See exactly how a function call travels across a network — marshalled into bytes, transmitted over the wire, unmarshalled on the server, executed, and returned as a response. All in your browser, no server needed.
concepts: Marshalling converts function name + arguments into a transmittable byte format (JSON, Protobuf, Thrift)
A stub/proxy makes a remote call look identical to a local function call from the caller's perspective
The wire protocol defines the byte layout — gRPC uses HTTP/2 + Protobuf; REST uses HTTP + JSON
Unmarshalling reconstructs the original call on the server, executes it, and marshals the response back
Real RPC frameworks (gRPC, Thrift, Cap'n Proto) auto-generate stub code from an interface definition

layers.label: Client
layers.description: The entry point. Accepts user input (a Linux command string) and displays the final output. Represents the caller in the RPC model.

layers.label: Client Stub
layers.description: Generated code that marshals the typed request into a protobuf message and unmarshals the typed response. Hides transport details.

layers.label: RPC Runtime
layers.description: Client-side transport machinery. Encodes the protobuf into HTTP/2 HEADERS + DATA frames (outbound) and decodes the HTTP/2 response (inbound).

layers.label: RPC Runtime
layers.description: Server-side transport machinery. Receives HTTP/2 frames and decodes the request (inbound); encodes the response into HTTP/2 DATA + TRAILERS (outbound).

layers.label: Server Stub
layers.description: Generated server glue. Deserializes the protobuf request, dispatches to the service implementation, and serializes the typed response.

layers.label: Server
layers.description: The actual service implementation. Executes the command and returns the result. Business logic lives here.

steps.note: Client enters command — box 1 activates, shows typed command
Client Stub marshals request — shows RunCommandRequest protobuf on left side
RPC Runtime encodes HTTP/2 — shows HEADERS + DATA frame on left side
Network transmit (forward) — animated packet crosses network bridge; networkAudio plays
Server RPC Runtime receives — shows same HTTP/2 inbound tokens on left side
Server Stub unmarshals — shows decoded RunCommandRequest + dispatch label on left
Server executes — runs command; typing audio plays; pauseMs=2000 for perceived processing
Server Stub marshals response — right side shows RunCommandResponse protobuf; boxes 5+6 turn 'received'
Server RPC Runtime encodes response — right side shows HTTP/2 DATA + TRAILERS
Network transmit (return) — animated packet crosses back; networkAudio plays
Client RPC Runtime decodes — right side shows HTTP/2 response tokens; boxes 3-6 become 'received'
Client Stub unmarshals response — right side shows RunCommandResponse + 'stdout -> caller' label
Client receives output — box 1 shows command + newline + output lines; all boxes 'received'

wireFormat.framingNote: Each gRPC message has a 1-byte compression flag + 4-byte length prefix before the protobuf bytes

tokenBuilders._note: Describes the token arrays ({kind, value}) rendered inside each box at each animation step. Token kinds: command|output|key|string|dim|punct|newline|indent

final: ... additional output lines separated by newline tokens ...

rpcRuntimeClient.left: buildHttp2RequestTokens(cmd) — HTTP/2 HEADERS block + DATA block with RunCommandRequest protobuf bytes
rpcRuntimeClient.right: buildHttp2ResponseTokens(cmd) — DATA block with RunCommandResponse protobuf bytes + TRAILERS block with grpc-status: 0

rpcRuntimeServer.left: same as rpcRuntimeClient.left (identical HTTP/2 ingress tokens)
rpcRuntimeServer.right: same as rpcRuntimeClient.right (identical HTTP/2 response tokens)

protoPanel.description: Separate collapsible panel showing the .proto contract

formatMessageHelper.description: Shared helper that renders a protobuf message shape as tokens

boxStatuses.idle: Default state — muted border, muted background
boxStatuses.waiting: Active in current forward pass — accent border, accent LED blinks
boxStatuses.received: Step completed — green/highlight tint, steady LED

audio.notes: beep plays on each step transition; network plays during network-forward and network-return; typing plays while server is processing (isRemoteProcessing)

Client.title: Client
Client.body: The client code calls a generated **gRPC** method as if it were local. It sends a **RunCommandRequest** and eventually receives a **RunCommandResponse**.
Client.more: In **gRPC**, the client usually works with a generated **strongly typed API** rather than manually building HTTP requests. For a **unary call** like this one, the client prepares a single **protobuf request**, hands it to the client stub, and then waits for one protobuf response. **Deadlines**, **metadata**, **retries**, and **authentication** concerns are typically attached at this layer or just below it, even though the calling code still looks like a normal method invocation.

Client Stub.title: Client Stub
Client Stub.body: The generated **client stub** accepts a typed request object, starts the **RPC**, and hides **serialization** and transport details from the caller.
Client Stub.more: This layer is where the method signature from the **.proto** file becomes usable application code. The stub knows the fully qualified **RPC path**, the request and response **protobuf types**, and how to pass those typed messages into the gRPC library for **marshalling** and **unmarshalling**. In many gRPC implementations this is also where **interceptors** hook in for logging, tracing, auth, or retries before the call reaches the transport runtime.

RPC Runtime.title: RPC Runtime
RPC Runtime.body: The **gRPC runtime** serializes **protobuf** messages into the gRPC wire format and carries them over **HTTP/2** using **HEADERS**, **DATA**, and **TRAILERS**.
RPC Runtime.more: This is the **transport machinery** that makes gRPC distinct from a generic RPC sketch. A **unary request** is carried as **initial metadata** in HTTP/2 headers plus a gRPC message inside the DATA frame, and the response comes back with its own DATA plus **trailing metadata** such as **grpc-status**. Each gRPC message adds a **1-byte compression flag** and **4-byte length prefix** before the protobuf bytes. **Flow control**, **stream management**, **metadata propagation**, compression flags, and status handling all live conceptually in this layer.

Server Stub.title: Server Stub
Server Stub.body: The generated **server handler** receives the decoded request from the **gRPC runtime**, dispatches to the service method, and returns the typed response for encoding back onto the wire.
Server Stub.more: On the server side, generated **glue code** bridges transport and business logic. The runtime reads **HTTP/2**, parses **gRPC framing**, deserializes the protobuf message, and then hands a typed request to the right **RPC handler**. The handler returns a typed response, which the runtime then serializes back onto the wire. **Validation**, **interceptors**, auth checks, and request context propagation often pass through here before the actual service implementation runs.

Server.title: Server
Server.body: This is the real server-side implementation of the **gRPC service**. **Business logic** runs here and produces the response message.
Server.more: This layer owns the actual **application behavior**. By the time execution reaches here, transport details such as **HTTP/2 framing** and **protobuf decoding** have already been handled, so the service can focus on using request fields and returning a typed response. In a real system, this is where **domain logic**, database access, downstream service calls, and error mapping to **gRPC status codes** would typically happen.

Proto Definition.title: Proto Definition
Proto Definition.body: This is the **.proto contract**. It defines the **gRPC service method** and the **protobuf** request and response message shapes shared by client and server.
Proto Definition.more: The **.proto file** is the **shared source of truth** for both sides of a gRPC system. From this contract, tooling generates **stubs**, **handlers**, and message classes in different languages, which is why client and server can agree on method names, field numbers, and serialization formats. **Field numbering** matters for wire compatibility, and this contract is what lets teams evolve APIs safely while preserving **backward compatibility**.

guide.title: RPC (Remote Procedure Call)

lines: **RPC** is a way one computer (like your local machine) calls another computer (often in the cloud) to run a function as if it were local, usually because the remote system hosts the service or resources.
In **system design**, **RPC** matters because remote calls introduce **latency**, **failures**, and **costs**, so you must balance local vs remote execution.
In **agentic computing**, agents use **RPC-style interfaces** to call external **tools**, **APIs**, and **execution environments** instead of doing everything locally.
introduction.simulationLine: In this simulation, you can enter a **Linux command** and watch it traverse the **local** and **remote** stack using **gRPC** protocol.

paragraphs: gRPC is an open source, high-performance RPC framework that lets a client call a remote method with a local-looking API. In practice, the contract starts with a **.proto** file, from which tooling generates **client stubs** and **server handlers**. That is why this demo separates the caller, stubs, runtime, and server implementation instead of treating the whole exchange as one generic request.

The transport is also more specific than a plain JSON API. gRPC commonly uses **HTTP/2** for transport and **Protocol Buffers** for message encoding, which is why the runtime boxes here show **HEADERS**, **DATA**, and **TRAILERS** rather than made-up RPC frames. Unary calls like this one usually send one protobuf request message and receive one protobuf response message, along with a final gRPC status.

This matters because gRPC is built for real distributed systems concerns such as **streaming**, **flow control**, **authentication**, **deadlines**, and **cancellation**. Even in a simple demo, keeping the protobuf contract, generated stubs, and HTTP/2 transport visible helps explain why gRPC feels different from a basic request-response sketch.

code.requestPayloadHint: [gRPC message: 1-byte compression flag + 4-byte length + RunCommandRequest protobuf bytes]
code.responsePayloadHint: [gRPC message: 1-byte compression flag + 4-byte length + RunCommandResponse protobuf bytes]
